definition: The Kalecki-Levy profit equation shows fiscal deficits mechanically support corporate earnings. The US 6-7% deficit contributes an estimated $20-40/share of S&P 500 EPS (7-15%). Any fiscal consolidation sufficient to stabilize debt/GDP would directly reduce the earnings base, creating a consolidation paradox where fiscal improvement triggers earnings recession.
evidence: The Kalecki-Levy accounting identity is definitional
European austerity 2011-2014: EPS fell 10-15%, multiples barely rose, indices flat to down
$20-40/share estimate has wide uncertainty (±50%) but direction is high-confidence
Identified as 'single most valuable insight across both analyses' in pair_1 debate
open_questions: What is the precise magnitude of the EPS impact per percentage point of deficit reduction?
Can monetary easing offset the earnings headwind from consolidation?
Does the paradox create a political economy trap that prevents consolidation entirely?
